title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 1
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: Your local council has asked you to write a report on volunteering activities in your area. Your report should describe the types of volunteering currently taking place, assess the impact on the community, identify any challenges volunteers and organisations face, and recommend ways the council could better support volunteering.
tasks.modelAnswer:
VOLUNTEERING IN THE BOROUGH OF THORNWICK: A REPORT

Introduction
This report provides an overview of volunteering activity in the Borough of Thornwick, evaluates its community impact, examines existing challenges, and recommends steps the council can take to strengthen voluntary engagement.

Current Volunteering Activity
The borough supports a diverse volunteering ecosystem. Key activities include food bank distribution, staffed by over 200 regular volunteers; environmental maintenance through the Green Thornwick initiative; befriending programmes for isolated elderly residents; and mentoring schemes in secondary schools. Between them, these activities contribute an estimated 15,000 volunteer hours per month.

Community Impact
The impact is substantial and multifaceted. Food banks have prevented food insecurity for hundreds of families, while befriending programmes have demonstrably reduced loneliness among older residents. School mentoring has yielded measurable improvements in attendance and attainment among participating pupils. These outcomes supplement statutory services in ways that generate significant savings for the public purse.

Challenges
Organisations consistently report difficulty retaining volunteers beyond an initial period, citing a lack of meaningful recognition and inadequate coordination support. Many smaller groups operate without administrative resources, limiting their capacity to recruit, train, and manage volunteers effectively.

Recommendations
The council should establish a central volunteering hub offering training, coordination support, and recognition events. A small grants scheme for emerging community organisations would also enable new groups to develop sustainable structures. Promoting employer-supported volunteering days through local business networks would further expand the volunteer base.

Conclusion
With modest but targeted council support, Thornwick's voluntary sector has the potential to grow significantly and deepen its community impact.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt: You read an article claiming that young people today are less community-minded and less likely to volunteer than previous generations. You disagree strongly with this view. Write a letter to the editor of the publication, challenging the claim, providing evidence of young people's contribution to their communities, and suggesting what could be done to encourage even greater youth participation in volunteering.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Editor,

I am writing in response to your recent article asserting that young people are increasingly indifferent to community life and unlikely to engage in volunteering. As a youth worker with ten years of experience, I find this characterisation both inaccurate and unhelpful, and I would like to offer a more evidence-based perspective.

The claim that young people are less community-minded than previous generations is not supported by the data. According to the National Council for Voluntary Organisations, the 18-24 age group is one of the fastest-growing segments of the volunteer workforce, with participation rates that have increased steadily over the past decade. Young people are at the forefront of environmental activism, community food growing, digital inclusion initiatives, and peer mental health support networks. To suggest otherwise is to overlook a generation actively engaged with the challenges of its time.

It is worth acknowledging, however, that young people face real structural barriers to formal volunteering: precarious employment, heavy academic workloads, and limited transport options. These constraints do not reflect a lack of willingness; they reflect the material conditions in which many young people operate.

If your publication genuinely wishes to support youth engagement, I would encourage it to advocate for flexible, short-term volunteering opportunities that fit around demanding schedules, and for schools and colleges to embed community participation into curricula.

I trust future coverage will take a more balanced and encouraging approach to this generation's considerable contributions.

Yours faithfully,
Dr Clare Mwangi
Youth Development Coordinator

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: A community engagement magazine is asking readers to review books, films, or programmes that explore the theme of volunteering or community service. Write a review describing the work, evaluating how effectively it portrays volunteering, and recommending it to others.
tasks.modelAnswer:
ORDINARY PEOPLE, EXTRAORDINARY IMPACT: A REVIEW OF 'VOLUNTEERING MATTERS'

'Volunteering Matters' is a six-part documentary series produced for public broadcasting that profiles volunteers working across a range of community settings in the United Kingdom. Each episode focuses on a different sector — from hospital befriending to conservation work — and follows a small group of volunteers over the course of several weeks.

The series succeeds admirably in humanising volunteering. Rather than presenting it as a virtuous abstraction, it shows the messy, sometimes frustrating, frequently rewarding reality of the work. One particularly memorable episode features a group of former prisoners engaged in urban woodland restoration, whose transformation over the course of their involvement is both moving and convincingly authentic. The production avoids sentimentality, allowing the subjects to speak for themselves without editorial manipulation.

The series also excels at illustrating the systemic importance of voluntary labour. By interviewing service managers alongside the volunteers themselves, it contextualises individual contributions within the wider landscape of public service provision — a perspective that is rarely communicated effectively in popular media.

A minor weakness is the series' geographical concentration on urban communities in England and Wales, which means rural and international volunteering contexts receive little attention.

Nevertheless, 'Volunteering Matters' is an exemplary piece of social documentary filmmaking. It is likely to inspire viewers to seek out volunteering opportunities of their own, which is arguably the highest compliment one can pay to a series of this kind. Warmly recommended for community groups, classrooms, and general audiences alike.
